question:
Assertion: $\left[\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{OH}_{2}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{-}$ form octahedral and tetrahedral structures.

Reason: Being electron deficient, boron readily reacts with Lewis bases like $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}$ The correct option among the following is
explanation:
Assertion is false because $\left[\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ does not exist, while $\left[\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{-}$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ is hybridised and have tetrahedral complex.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958372/preptracker/questions/q_107877_explanation_1764958371844.png
The boron atom is electron deficient. It has 6 electrons instead of 8 electrons in its valence shell. Hence, it acts as a Lewis acid by readily accepting the lone pair from oxygen from a $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule or $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ion. Hence, Reason is true.

options.text: Assertion is true, Reason is true and Reason is the correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is true, Reason is true but is not the correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

question: A and B are formed when borax is dissolved in water. C and B are formed when borax is reacted with aqueous HCl solution. What are A and C respectively?
explanation:
The reaction of borax $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}\right)$ with water occurs as follows:
${\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{4}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{7}}}{\text{ + }}\,\,{\text{7}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {{\text{4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{({\text{B}})} {\text{ + }}\,\,\mathop {{\text{2NaOH}}}\limits_{({\text{A}})} $
Borax reacts with HCl (dil.) as follows :
${\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{4}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{7}}}{\text{ + }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{2HCl }}{\text{.10}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to $
$\mathop {{\text{4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{(B)}}} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} + \mathop {{\text{2NaCl}}}\limits_{{\text{(C)}}} {\mkern 1mu} + {\mkern 1mu} 5{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
Hence, $(\mathrm{A})=\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $(\mathrm{C})=\mathrm{NaCl}$
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

question:
Pick the correct statement.
I. Borax is white crystalline solid containing $\left[\mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ units.
II. Aqueous solution of borax is acidic in nature.
III. Cobalt gives blue colour in borax bead test.
explanation:
I. Structural formula of borax $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ is
$\mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right] \cdot 8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \Rightarrow$ correct statement.
II. Aqueous solution of borax is basic in nature, because borax in water dissociates into $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{BO}_{3}$ (weak acid) and NaOH (strong base).
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{BO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NaOH}$
$\Rightarrow$ Incorrect statement.
III. Cobalt (Co) gives blue colour of cobalt metaborate $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{BO}_{2}\right)_{2}\right]$ in borax bead $\left(\mathrm{NaBO}_{2}+\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ test $\Rightarrow$ Correct statement.
So, the correct statements are (I) and (III).

explanation:
The most abundant isotope of boron is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{{ }_{5}^{11} \mathrm{~B}}}}$.

(i) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~B}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{BN}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}}$
(ii) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{4 \mathrm{~B}_{(\mathrm{s})}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3(\mathrm{~s})}}}}$
Oxidation state of boron in both BN and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}}}$ is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{+3(\mathrm{y})}}}$
So, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{x+y=6+3=9}}}$
So, the correct option is (4)

question: The strongest Lewis acid among boron halides is
explanation: Generally, as the electronegativity increases, acidic character increases, but in case of boron halides, $\mathrm{BI}_{3}$ is most acidic, i.e. strongest Lewis acid.This is because there is back donation of electrons from halogen atom to boron in$\mathrm{BX}_{3}(\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{I})$This makes the $\mathrm{BX}_{3}$ less electron deficient. The tendency of back donation decreases as the size of halogen atom increases (due to large energy difference).Hence, due to large size of $\mathrm{I}, \mathrm{BI}_{3}$ is most electron deficient and thus, the strongest Lewis acid.

question: Carbon forms two oxides which have different compositions. The equivalent mass of which remains constant?
explanation: The equivalent mass of a substance have different value under different conditions, it varies with valency. Let us consider the two oxides of carbon viz. $$\mathrm{CO}$$ and $$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$$. In $$\mathrm{CO}_{2} 12$$ parts of carbon combine with 16 parts of oxygen thus equivalent mass of carbon in $$\mathrm{CO}$$ is $$\dfrac{12 \times 8}{16}=6.0$$. In $$\mathrm{CO}_{2}, 12$$ parts of carbon combine with 32 parts of oxygen, thus the equivalent mass of carbon in this oxide is $$\dfrac{12 \times 8}{32}=3.0$$

$$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{\text { White ppt }}{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2} \\& \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}\end{aligned}$$
Hence, the gas ${\text{A}}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{\text{B}}$ are $$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$$ and $$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$$ respectivley.

question:
Assertion: Among the carbon allotropes, diamond is an insulator, whereas, graphite is a good conductor of electricity.


Reason: Hybridization of carbon in diamond and graphite are ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}\,$ and ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{2}}}$, respectively.
explanation:
In diamond, each C-atom is covalently bonded to four other $$\mathrm{C}$$-atoms to give a tetrahedral unit. In diamond each C-atom is ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}$ hybridized. Therefore each $$\mathrm{C}$$-atom forms four sigma bonds with neighbouring $$\mathrm{C}$$-atoms. In diamond each $$\mathrm{C}$$-atom utilizes its four unpaired electrons in bond formation.
These bonding electrons are localized. Due to this reason diamond is a bad conductor of electricity. In graphite each C-atom is covalently bonded to three $$\mathrm{C}$$-atoms to give trigonal geometry. Each C-atom in graphite is ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{2}}}$ hybridized. Three out of four valence electrons of each C-atom are used in bond formation while the fourth electron is free to move in the structure of graphite. Due to this reason graphite is a good conductor of electricity.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
In the reaction I and II, the covalencies of Be and Al in X and Y are respectively
I. $\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+\underset{\text { (Excess) }}{\mathrm{NaOH}} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$
II. $\quad \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\underset{\text { (Excess) }}{\mathrm{NaOH}} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}$
explanation:
In reaction $\mathrm{I}, \mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]$ is formed.
${\text{Be}}{({\text{OH}})_2} + \mathop {{\text{NaOH}}}\limits_{{\text{ (Excess) }}} \to {\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}\mathop {\left[ {{\text{Be}}{{({\text{OH}})}_4}} \right]}\limits_{{\text{ (X) }}} $
$\therefore$ Covalency of Be in $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Be}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]$ is 4 .
In reaction II, $\mathrm{Na}\left[\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]$ is formed.
$\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\underset{\text { (Excess) }}{\mathrm{NaOH}} \rightarrow \underset{\text { (Y) }}{\mathrm{Na}\left[\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]}$
$\therefore$ Covalency of Al in the complex is 4 .

question: The correct order of Lewis acidic character of boron trihalides is
explanation:
The order of back-bonding in boron halides is $\mathrm{BF}_{3}>\mathrm{BCl}_{3}>\mathrm{BBr}_{3}>\mathrm{BI}_{3}$ due to the strength of overlapping which is in order
${\text{2p}} - {\text{2p}} > {\text{2p}} - {\text{3p}} > {\text{2p}} - {\text{4p}} > {\text{2p}} - {\text{5p}}$
Hence, stronger is the back-bonding, lesser the availability of empty p-orbital and thus, weaker the tendency to act as a Lewis acid.
So, the correct order of Lewis acidic character of boron trihalides is $\mathrm{BI}_{3}>\mathrm{BBr}_{3}>\mathrm{BCl}_{3}>\mathrm{BF}_{3}$

question:
The correct statements from the following are
(I) The decreasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Tl}>\mathrm{In}>\mathrm{Ga}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{B}}}}$.
(II) Down the group 13, electronegativity decreases from top to bottom.
(III) Al dissolves in dil. HCl and liberates ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}$ but conc. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}}}}$ renders Al passive by forming a protective oxide layer on the surface.
(IV) All elements of group 13 exhibits highly stable +1 oxidation state.
(V) Hybridisation of Al in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}}}}$ ion is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}}}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
(I) The decreasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Tl}>\mathrm{In}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Ga}>\mathrm{B}}}}$. The presence of additional 10 d -electrons offer poor screening for the outer electron in gallium. As a result the atomic radius of Ga is lesser than that of Al.
(II) Down the group electronegativity first decreases from B to Al and then increases marginally. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{B}(2.0), \mathrm{Al}(1.5), \mathrm{Ga}(1.6)}}}$, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{In}(1.7)}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Tl}(1.8)}}}$
(IV) The relative stability of +1 oxidation state progressively increases for heavier elements.Thus in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Tl},+1}}}$ oxidation state is predominant and for Al it is +3 . So, +1 oxidation state is highly stable for Tl only.
So, the correct option is (3)

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953667/preptracker/questions/q_20621_explanation_1764953666854.png The ionisation enthalpy decreases from B to Al due to increase in atomic size. The ionisation energy of Ga is slightly higher than that of Al due to ineffective screening of 3 d -electrons.

question: Choose the correct statement from the following with reference to inert pair effect.
explanation:
Inert pair effect is less prominent in Sn than in Pb . Therefore, $ + 2$ oxidation state of Sn is less stable than its $ + 4$ oxidation state. Hence, $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ can easily lose two electrons to form $\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}$ and thus $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ acts as a reducing agent.
$\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Sn}^{4+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
In $\mathrm{Pb},+2$ oxidation state of Pb is more stable than $ + 4$ oxidation state due to inert pair effect.
Therefore, $\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}$ can easily gain two electrons to form $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ and hence acts as an oxidising agent.
$\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$

options.text: $\mathrm{Sn}^{+4}$ is reducing agent but $\mathrm{Pb}^{+4}$ is an oxidising agent.
$\mathrm{Sn}^{+4}$ is an oxidising agent but $\mathrm{Pb}^{+2}$ is a reducing agent.
$\mathrm{Sn}^{+2}$ is reducing agent but $\mathrm{Pb}^{+4}$ is an oxidising agent.
$\mathrm{Sn}^{+2}$ is an oxidising agent but $\mathrm{Pb}^{+4}$ is reducing agent.

question:
Identify the correct statements from the following:
I. Quartz is a piezoelectric material.
II. All group 14 tetrachlorides except $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$ are easily hydrolysed by water
III. The C-C bond distance within the layer of graphite is 154 pm .
IV. $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ is soluble in aqueous HCl solution.
explanation:
I. Some types of quartz crystals have piezoelectric properties. They develop an electric potential upon the application of mechanical stress. Hence, statement (I) is correct.
II. All group 14 tetrachloride except $\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$ are easily hydrolysed by water. It is because of nonavailability of vacant ${\text{d}}$ - orbitals and steric crowding due to presence of four Cl-atoms around C -atom which do not allow water molecule to approach antibonding orbitals of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bonds. Hence, statement (II) is correct.
III. In graphite, the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond distance within the layer of graphite is 141.5 pm . Hence, statement (III) is incorrect.
IV. $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ is insoluble in aqueous HCl solution. Hence, statement (IV) is incorrect.

question:
Consider the following statements about group 13 elements.

The incorrect statements are
explanation:
Among the given statements, C and D are incorrect.
(C) In case of aluminium, the sum of first three ionisation energies decreases considerably and hence, its value can never be positive. Its $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{V})$ value is $ - 1.66$ (i.e. in negative).
(D) In group 13, the $ + 1$ oxidation state becomes more and more stable as we move down the group i.e. $\mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Ga} < \mathrm{In} < \mathrm{Tl}$. This is because of the inert pair effect. Thus, in Tl, $ + 1$ oxidation state is more predominant.

question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct for group 13 elements?
A. Al reacts with dil. HCl to liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas B.
B. Al reacts with conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ to liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas
C. Boron reacts with only acids to liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas
D. Anhydrous reacts with moisture to liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas
explanation:
A. When Al reacts with dil. HCl , it liberates $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas. $2 \mathrm{Al}+6 \mathrm{HCl}$ (dil.) $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}$
B. Al reacts with conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and liberates NO gas. $\mathrm{Al}+4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ (conc.) $\rightarrow \mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3}+\mathrm{NO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
C. Crystalline boron does not react with boiling $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{HI}$ etc.
D. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ reacts with moisture and form aluminium hydroxide.
$\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+3 \mathrm{HCl}$
Hence, option (4) is correct.
